coredata.dc:description: Herein, a novel ultrasensitive cholesterol photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensor was designed based on fluorinated molecularly imprinted polymers (FMIPs) functionalized CH3NH3PbI3@carbon nanodots (CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs) platform. 2-(Perfluorooctyl) ethyl acrylate (PFEA), a functional monomer, was chosen not only to endow CH3NH3PbI3 good selectivity but also improve its aqueous stability because of the fluorine containing long hydrophobic chain. Furthermore, the FMIPs can inhibit the recombination of electron holes and prolonged the carrier life time, and dramatically enhance the charge transfer efficiency by the reduction of defect density through the polymer passivation of grain boundaries of perovskite. The morphology, the enhanced hydrophobicity and photoelectric properties of FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs interface was characterized by transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectrum, scanning electron microscope, contact angle, photoluminescence spectrum and intensity modulated photovoltage spectrum, respectively. Under the optimized conditions, the linear range of the designed PEC sensor for cholesterol detection was 1.0 × 10−13–1.0 × 10−9 mol/L, and the detection limit was down to 8.9 × 10−14 mol/L. The new PEC sensor exhibited specific selectivity, good stability and hypersensitivity, and was successfully applied to cholesterol detection in human serum samples. This work extended the development of the MIPs based sensor and the high-performance perovskite.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, a novel ultrasensitive cholesterol photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensor was designed based on fluorinated molecularly imprinted polymers (FMIPs) functionalized CH3NH3PbI3@carbon nanodots (CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs) platform. 2-(Perfluorooctyl) ethyl acrylate (PFEA), a functional monomer, was chosen not only to endow CH3NH3PbI3 good selectivity but also improve its aqueous stability because of the fluorine containing long hydrophobic chain. Furthermore, the FMIPs can inhibit the recombination of electron holes and prolonged the carrier life time, and dramatically enhance the charge transfer efficiency by the reduction of defect density through the polymer passivation of grain boundaries of perovskite. The morphology, the enhanced hydrophobicity and photoelectric properties of FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs interface was characterized by transmission electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectrum, scanning electron microscope, contact angle, photoluminescence spectrum and intensity modulated photovoltage spectrum, respectively. Under the optimized conditions, the linear range of the designed PEC sensor for cholesterol detection was 1.0 × 10−13–1.0 × 10−9 mol/L, and the detection limit was down to 8.9 × 10−14 mol/L. The new PEC sensor exhibited specific selectivity, good stability and hypersensitivity, and was successfully applied to cholesterol detection in human serum samples. This work extended the development of the MIPs based sensor and the high-performance perovskite.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Cholesterol is an essential lipid that not only is involved in the formation of cell membranes and plays an important role in ensuring the membrane integrity and fluidity, but also a precursor to a variety of biological compounds such as vitamin D, hormones and bile acids [1–5]. The blood cholesterol level for a healthy person is approximately 5.2 mmol L−1 (200 mg dL−1) [2,6]. High level of cholesterol in the blood can lead to atherosclerosis, coronary heart disease, myocardial infarction, hypertension, diabetes and other cardiovascular diseases [2,6,7]. On the other hand, the deficiency of cholesterol can cause depression, cancer, sepsis, low cholesterol lipoprotein, hyperthyroidism, anemia, malnutrition and other diseases that harm human health [1,8,9]. Considering the huge importance of maintaining human cholesterol level within a certain range, the thorough control of its level via easily accessible analytical methods is an urgent public health issue. Rapid, sensitive and accurate measurement of cholesterol content is of great significance for the prevention, diagnosis and treatment of diseases.

Common approaches for cholesterol detection are fluorimetry [2,5,8], colorimetry [10–12], high performances liquid chromatography [13–15], spectrophotometry [15,16], electrochemiluminescence [17–19], electrophoresis [20,21] and electrochemistry [22–24]. Photoelectrochemical (PEC) sensors not only have the advantages of traditional electrochemical sensors, such as simplicity, rapidity, low cost, high selectivity, long-term monitoring and online detection, etc., but also show the superiority of low background signal and high sensitivity owing to the complete separation of light and electricity [25–27].

For designing an excellent PEC sensor, the photoactive species and the recognition elements are considered to be the two main factors. Organic-inorganic perovskite materials (OIPMs) have unique and nearly perfect photoelectric properties such as direct band gap, large absorption coefficient, long electron-hole diffusion length, etc., which opens up another energetic spring for the research of photoelectric devices [28,29]. Water and thermal instability are the main obstacles to the commercialization of OIPMs, so the exploration of stable perovskite materials and the preparation of full coverage and uniform perovskite films have attracted the attention of researchers [30–32]. Polymer encapsulation is the simplest and most efficient method to improve the stability of OIPMs. Fluorinated organic materials are widely used to enhance the performance of perovskite for three reasons: First, the fluorinated compounds show high thermal and oxidation stability because of the strong CF bond; Second, they have excellent hydrophobicity, which can effectively enhance the moisture resistance of perovskites by external packaging with them; Third, fluorine substitution in organic materials can regulate the arrangement of energy levels, optimize the morphology of perovskite films, passivate surface and boundary defects, and improve the photoelectric performance of perovskite [33–35]. In our previous work, the hydrophobic polymer polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) was successfully applied to the encapsulation of CH3NH3PbI3 to improve its stability [36].

It is well known that MIPs have predetermined selectivity for specific analytes, high stability under extreme chemical conditions, and can be reused [37,38]. On the other hand, it has been demonstrated in the literature that the incorporation of highly fluorinated alkyl chains into materials can effectively improve the stability to air and temperature due to the formation of a kinetic barrier to prevent the diffusion of O2 and/or H2O [39]. In addition, the polymers of perfluoroalkyl-substituted acrylates maintain both the special surface property of fluoropolymers and the good film-forming characteristic of poly(meth)acrylates [40]. In this study, we chose perfluorinated organic compounds of 2-(perfluorooctyl) ethyl acrylate (PFEA) as a functional monomer to prepare fluorinated molecularly imprinted polymers (FMIPs) to functionalize CH3NH3PbI3@carbon nanodots (CNDs), which can not only improve the long-term stability and moisture-proof property of perovskite, but also give it excellent selectivity [41,42]. FMIPs can inhibit electron-hole pair recombination, prolong the carrier lifetime, and dramatically enhance the charge transfer efficiency because the defect density is reduced by passivating polymer at perovskite grain boundaries. CNDs can promote the extraction of holes, reduced the recombination of electron-hole pairs, improve the stability of CH3NH3PbI3 in water phase and provide abundant loading sites [36,43,44]. Based on FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs, a new cholesterol PEC sensor with high stability, specific selectivity and supersensitivity was developed. The designed sensor has been successfully applied to the cholesterol detection in human serum, and the results were consistent well with those of commercial detection methods, showing its good practicability.

PbI2 (99 %), 2-(perfluorooctyl) ethyl acrylate (PFEA), triglyceride (TG), ethylene glycol dimethacrylate (EGDMA), and acetaminophen (AP) were bought from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Cholesterol (99 %), methylammonium iodide (CH3NH3I, MAI, 98.0 %) was obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Uric acid was purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. Methacrylic acid (MAA), styrene (ST), azodiisobutyronitrile (AIBN), N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF), ascorbic acid, glucose, glycine, lactic acid, urea, starch, hexane, ethanol and ethyl acetate were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The serum samples were provided by Subei People’s Hospital. All of the chemicals were used without further purification. The indium tin oxide coated glasses (ITO, 8 Ω/square) were purchased from Zhuhai Kaivo Optoelectronic Technology Co., Ltd (Zhuhai, China).

The photoelectrochemical experiments and intensity-modulated photovoltage spectroscopy (IMVS) were recorded on a CIMPS-02 workstation (Zennium, Zahner-ElektrikGmbH & CoKG, Germany) by using a white light (500 W, WLC02, (4300k)#1522, Zahner-Elektrik) as the visible irradiation source. The Mott-Schottky measurements, cyclic voltammetry (CV) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) were performed on a CHI660D electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument, China). A three-electrode system was adopted by using a modified ITO as the working electrode, a commercial saturated Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode and a Pt cylinder as the auxiliary electrode. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of CNDs were obtained from a transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100, Japan) at an acceleration voltage of 100 keV and a transform infrared spectrometer (TENSOR27, Germany), respectively. The morphologies of CH3NH3PbI3, CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs, FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs and non-imprinted polymers (NIPs)@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs were characterized by a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Zeiss Supra 55, Germany). The contact angles were surveyed by an OCA20 (Dataphysics, Germany). X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were performed on an XRD-7000 (Shimadzu, Japan) diffractometer with a Cu target radiation resource (λ = 1.5406 Å). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were operated on a spectrofluorometer FS5 (Edinburgh, England).

CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs was prepared as reported in our previous work [36]. Firstly, CNDs were synthesized by the simple hydrothermal method with grape juice being used as the carbon source. Specifically, 30 mL grape juice used as the carbon source was added into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 8 h. Then it was cooled naturally to room temperature when the reaction was over. The resulting solution was centrifuged subsequently at 3000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was taken and dialyzed with a dialysis membrane (molecular interception capacity 1000) for 24 h, and the dialyzed solution was further lyophilized for use. Finally, 0.9220 g PbI2, 0.3179 g MAI and 20 mg CNDs were dispersed in 2 mL DMF and followed with agitation in a water bath at 70 °C for 6 h. The obtained CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs precursor solution was collected for use.

FMIPs were prepared by a simple thermal polymerization process by applying PFEA, MAA and ST as the functional monomers, cholesterol as the template molecule, AIBN as the initiator, and EGDMA as the cross-linker, respectively. 395 μL (1.25 mmol) PFEA, 326 μL MAA (3.75 mmol) and 145 μL ST (1.25 mmol) were dissolved in 10.0 mL DMF. Then 0.3867 g (1.0 mmol) cholesterol was added, and the solution was shaken at 35 °C for 6 h. After that, 500 μL (2.7 mmol) EGDMA and 6.5 mg (40 μmol) AIBN were added to the above solution, which was then passed through nitrogen at room temperature for 30 min to obtain the FMIPs precursor solution. As a control, the NIPs precursor solution was prepared in the same manner without the presence of template molecule cholesterol.

The two precursor solutions prepared above of FMIPs (225 μL) and 2 mL CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs were mixed and then oscillated the mixture violently for 5 min. Next, 15 μL of the mixed solution was added to the surface of ITO (1 cm2 of conductive area), which was pre-cleaned in turn with toluene, acetone, ethanol and ultrapure water for 20 min, respectively. After drying in an oven at 60 °C for 6 h, the FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO was obtained. For comparison, the NIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO was prepared in the same way by depositing a mixture of NIPs precursor solution and CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs precursor solution on ITO. The template molecules were eluted with hexane for 15 min. Scheme 1 displays the schematic illustration of the FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO PEC sensor preparation process and the cholesterol detection mechanism. When FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs was excited by photons with energies equal to or higher than that of their band gap, the photogenerated electron-hole pairs were efficiently separated. Subsequently, the photogenerated electrons were captured by dissolved oxygen to form superoxide anion (O2 •−) and then be oxidized at the counter electrode. Holes were easily captured by the electrons of the ITO electrode in the circuit to generate cathodic photocurrent.

The photoelectrochemical detection of cholesterol was performed in 0.1 mol/L Na2SO4 solution at the bias potential of 0.0 V under visible light irradiation. Firstly, the original photocurrent of FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO was measured and denoted as I0. Then, the electrode was placed in ethyl acetate solution of cholesterol with different concentrations and stirred continuously for 15 min at 750 RPM. Next, the electrode was removed and placed in 0.1 mol/L Na2SO4 to detect the photocurrent again and labeled as IS. The quantification of cholesterol was achieved based on the current change of ΔI = I0–IS. For the analysis of cholesterol in real samples, 100 μL of serum was firstly added to 1.0 mL ethyl acetate, and then diluted serially with ethyl acetate. The recovery rate of cholesterol was tested by spiking cholesterol standard at different concentrations to the blank serum matrix of the diluted serums.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of different materials and the developed PEC sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows (A) TEM image and (B) FT-IR spectrum of the as-fabricated CNDs. It can be seen from Fig. 1A that the size of CNDs was uniform, and the average particle size was about 5–8 nm. In Fig. 1B, three characteristic absorption peaks were present, namely, the typical OH peak at 3240–3670 cm−1, the CO stretching vibration peak at 1640 cm−1, and the − COOH stretching vibration peak at 1460 cm−1, which proved the existence of these functional groups in CNDs [45–47]. These functional groups can passivate the grain boundaries of perovskite and improve its moisture resistance [48,49].

SEM was employed for the characterization of the morphologies of CH3NH3PbI3, CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs, FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs and NIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs. As shown in Fig. 2 A, there were large defects and many pinholes among the original perovskite grains [50,51]. After the introduction of CNDs (Fig. 2B), the grain boundary defects of perovskite were significantly reduced, which was because the OH of CNDs effectively passivated the unstable lead ions on the edge of perovskite, thus improving the stability of perovskite in water phase [52,53]. When the FMIPs were combined, the formed film was very different from that before. It can be clearly seen in Fig. 2C that its surface was uneven and composed of many small particles, which was more conducive to the recognition and detection of target molecules. Fig. 2D displays the morphology of NIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs, which was similar to that of CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs, whereas was different from that of FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs, indicating the successful imprinting of cholesterol on FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs film.

The wettability of the material was measured by the contact angle. As shown in Fig. 3 , the contact angles of CH3NH3PbI3, CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs, FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs and NIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs were 46°, 73°, 98° and 86°, respectively. The larger the contact angle, the better the moisture resistance of the material, that is, the better the stability in the water phase [54,55]. The results suggested that the combination of CNDs with CH3NH3PbI3 improved the moisture resistance of perovskite. The contact angles of FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs and NIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs were both improved, which should be attributed to the long hydrophobic chain of PFEA. In addition, FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs presented a higher contact angle than NIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs owing to cholesterol being a hydrophobic lipid substance as well as the synergistic effect of cholesterol and PFEA. At the same time, it also demonstrated the successful introduction of cholesterol in the imprinting process, providing a possibility for the construction of sensors.

The stability of PEC sensor is a prerequisite for cholesterol detection and the practical application. In this work, I-t method was adopted to test the stability of different materials in 0.1 mol/L Na2SO4 electrolyte. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the photocurrent of CH3NH3PbI3/ITO (curve a) decayed rapidly, and reached 11.6 % of the initial current after 450 s. By the introduction of CNDs, the variation of the photocurrent at CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO (curve b) was significantly improved, and the photocurrent intensity was 84.6 % of the initial current after 450 s. It can be explained that CNDs passivated the grain boundary of perovskite, which increased the moisture resistance of perovskite. It was obviously seen that the photocurrent stability of FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO (curve c) was significantly superior to that of the first two materials. After 450 s, the photocurrent intensity maintained 99.4 % of the initial current ascribed to the hydrophobic long chain of PFEA, which prevented water from invading into the perovskite film. Additionally, the quantum efficiency is typically quantified by the incident photon-to-current conversion efficiencies (IPCEs) as the charge transport efficiency index, which was estimated according to the Eq. (1) [56]:
(1)
IPCE =
1240
× I
Plight × λ
×
100\%
where I is the photocurrent density (mA/cm2), λ is the wavelength of the incident light (nm), and Plight is the irradiation power density, respectively (mW/cm2). The quantum efficiency of FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO photoelectrode was calculated to be 3.37 %, confirming the light harvesting of FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs and built-in electronic levels generation, and subsequently efficient separation and transportation of photoexcited charges.

XRD patterns (Fig. 4B) were used to analyze the crystal structures of CH3NH3PbI3 (curve a), CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs (curve b), FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs (curve c), and NIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs (curve d). As it shown, for all four materials, four strong diffraction peaks appeared at 14.15°, 28.51°, 31.82° and 40.48° corresponding to (110), (220), (310) and (224) the four crystal faces of CH3NH3PbI3 tetrahedral structure, respectively. It indicated that that the introduction of CNDs, FMIPs and NIPs did not destroy the crystal structure of CH3NH3PbI3 [57,58].

PL emission spectroscopies were used to investigate the influence of CNDs and FMIPs on the charge separation efficiency of CH3NH3PbI3. The lower peak intensity indicated the lower rate of electron-hole recombination, that is, the separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers was higher [59]. As shown in Fig. 4C, at the excitation wavelength of 550 nm, the PL intensity of CH3NH3PbI3 (curve a) was the strongest, meaning the most lattice defects and the highest electron-hole recombination rate [60]. Compared with CH3NH3PbI3, the PL intensity of CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs (curve b) was decreased, indicating that CNDs can reduce the lattice defects of CH3NH3PbI3 and increased its charge transfer efficiency [61]. When FMIPs were introduced (curve c), the PL intensity was dramatically decreased and the emission peak appeared blue shift, which suggested that the charge transfer efficiency was dramatically enhanced owing to the polymer passivated the grain boundary of CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs and lowered the defect density [62].

IMVS was used to calculate the carrier lifetime of the material. Fig. 4D displays the typical Nyquist diagrams of CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs (curve a) and FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs (curve b) with both of them showing as semicircles. According to the formula tr = 1/2π fmin (fmin is the minimum characteristic frequency of the IMVS imaginary component) [63], the electron lifetimes of CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs and FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs were calculated to be 186.47 ms and 269.38 ms, respectively. The significantly increased electron lifetime of CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs by the introduction of FMIPs indicated that it promoted the separation of photogenerated electrons, inhibited the recombination of electron hole pairs and thus prolonged the carrier lifetime [64].

The Mott-Schottky curve (Fig. 5 ) of FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO in 0.1 mol/L Na2SO4 solution was measured in the same standard three-electrode electrochemical cell as the electrochemical tests. The applied potential for Mott-Schottky curve varied from 0.7 V to 1.4 V, and the frequency scan was 1 kHz. The flat band potential (EFB) of FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs can be obtained using Mott-Schottky analysis by extending the linear section to 1/C2 = 0 [65–67]. The EFB value was obtained to be 1.15 V, vs Ag/AgCl. In addition, the negative slope of the linear part indicated that FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs exhibited p-type semiconductor properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of experimental conditions on the developed PEC sensor
ce:section.ce:para: In order to achieve the optimal performance of the constructed FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO PEC sensor, experimental conditions that may affect the sensor were optimized by using I-t and controlling variables method, such as the content of CNDs in perovskite, the mass ratio of imprinted polymer to perovskite precursor solution, the identification time, the elusion time and the pH of electrolyte. In the condition optimization, hexane was used as the eluent, and 1.0 × 10−10 mol/L cholesterol as the identification solution.

The concentration of CNDs in perovskite was critical because while CNDs can improve the stability of perovskite in the aqueous phase, excessive CNDs can reduce the photocurrent response of perovskite. Therefore, the influence of CNDs content on perovskite performance was firstly explored. As shown in Fig. 6 A, with the increase of CNDs content, the photocurrent change rate of perovskite first decreased and then increased. Here, the photocurrent change rate referred to ΔI (%)=(I1–I2)/I1 × 100 %, where I1 and I2 were the photocurrents at the first cycle and last cycle, respectively. It achieved the minimum when the content of CNDs in perovskite reached 1.0 mg/mL. Continuous increase of the CNDs content resulted in a gradual increase of the current change rate, which may be due to excessive CNDs leading to more pinholes in perovskite film. And when it exceeded 5.0 mg/mL, the current change rate remained basically unchanged. On the premise of ensuring the stability of perovskite, the content of CNDs was selected as 1.0 mg/mL.

The proportion of FMIPs is an important factor affecting the recognition effect of PEC sensor. Fig. 6B shows that the current change gradually increased as the FMIPs ratio increased, which can be ascribed to the increase in the number of imprinting sites. And the current change reached the maximum when the ratio increased to 10 %. As the FMIPs proportion continued to increase, the current change began to decrease gradually, which may be because the thickness of FMIPs on CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO increased and the charge transfer barrier increased, thus reducing the photoelectric response. Therefore, 10 % FMIPs was chosen as the optimal ratio.

The successful elution of template molecules from the imprinted membrane is another important factor affecting the recognition effect of PEC sensor. The template molecules were removed by destroying the hydrogen bond force between them and functional monomers using hexane as an eluent and the elution time being controlled within 3–18 min. As can be seen from Fig. 6C, when the elution time was 15 min, the change in photocurrent of the sensor reached the maximum value. And as the elution time continued to increase, the current difference before and after identification remained stable. Hence, the optimal elution time was set to15 min.

After the target molecules were removed from the imprinted film, the effective recognition and detection of them will affect the sensor performance. The recognition time on the photocurrent response in a range of 3–15 min was investigated by placing the as-prepared PEC sensor of FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO in an ethyl acetate solution with 1.0 × 10−10 mol/L cholesterol. It can be seen from Fig. 6D that the current changed mostly when the recognition time was 9 min. And it remained almost constant as the time continued to increase. That is, the recognition sites of cholesterol reached the maximum value, so 9 min was the best recognition time.

The effect of the electrolyte pH value on the photocurrent responses of the FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO was investigated for cholesterol in 0.1 M mol/L Na2SO4 with pH ranging from 4.0 to 9.0 as shown in Fig. 6E. It could be seen that the photocurrent changing value increased initially with the increase of pH and then decreased gradually. It indicated that the pH of the electrolyte had a strong influence on the performance of the PEC sensor. The maximum value was obtained when the solution pH was 7.0. Therefore, a value of pH 7.0 was chosen for all electrochemical experiments. From the data it was clear that the developed sensor of FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO was approximate for detecting cholesterol within the pH range of blood.

To further study the adsorption property of the FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO, the Scatchard plots for binding of cholesterol by the PEC sensor (Fig. 8 ) were treated according to the Scatchard equation [69]: S/[A] = –KA [A] + KAkc R, where S is the signal (i.e. ΔI) given by the proposed electrochemical sensor, [A] is the concentration of the substrate A (cholesterol), KA is the binding constant, k is the proportionality constant of the signal, c R is equivalent to the concentration of the sites occupied by the target cholesterol. As shown in Fig. 8, the plots illustrated the batch-type rebinding assay of FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO with cholesterol, and two linear components appeared in the graph, indicating the presence of two different binding sites (the specific binding and the non-specific binding) for cholesterol on the FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO. The specific binding constant KA was calculated to be 5.58 × 1012 L mol−1, which was about 64 times larger than that obtained by the NIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO (8.75 × 1010 L mol−1), suggesting that the adsorption mechanism predominating at the specific binding involved the imprinted sites. It could be attributed to the large quantities of imprinted cavities on the electrode modified surface, which could distinguish cholesterol from other interferents because of the particular cavity size, the functional group distribution, and the electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonds between cholesterol and the binding sits. Thus, the target cholesterol molecules could be specifically accumulated by FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO, while other interferents not complementary to the cavities can hardly be recognized and would remain in the solution.

Since human blood serum contains many inorganic and biological components which may interfere with the cholesterol detection, the selective nature of FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO towards photoelectrochemical sensing of cholesterol was investigated. The applied tolerance limit for the interfering species was the maximum concentration that gave a relative error of ± 5 % at a concentration of 1.0 × 10−10 mol/L cholesterol. From studies, 106-fold of the common inorganic ions such as Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, and Cl− had no intrusion in the analysis of cholesterol. 1000-fold of Fe2+, Fe3+, S2−, NO3 −, NO2 −, PO4 −, and HCO3 − had no effect on the determination of cholesterol. 400-fold of oxalate, citrate and ascorbate did not affect the determination of cholesterol. Similarly, glycine, glucose, uric acid, ascorbic acid, lactic acid, urea, and starch did not interfere up to 200-fold concentration. The given data clearly indicated that FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO possessed high sensitivity and selectivity, which can effectively detect cholesterol specifically from the mixture containing all possibly competitive interfering species.

Next, the reproducibility of the as-fabricated FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO was examined by measuring the photochemical responses of 1.0 × 10−10 mol/L cholesterol on five electrodes produced under the same conditions. A relative standard deviation (RSD) was calculated to be 5.27 %, showing an acceptable reproducibility. Through repeating detection five times of 1.0 × 10−10 mol/L cholesterol with the same FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO, the repeatability of the PEC sensor was estimated and the RSD was 3.89 %. The long-term stability was also studied to prove the practical applications feasibility of FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO. The photocurrent response of the sensor decrement was less than 4.33 % for the first week storage when it was stored dry at room temperature. After one-month storage period, the current decreased by only 7.51 % compared with the initial photocurrent response, indicating that the proposed sensor had the long-term stability and good enough for continual operation. It could be ascribed to the preparation of FMIPs by using perfluorinated organic compounds PFEA as one of the functional monomer, which improved perovskite water resistance owing to the fluorine containing long hydrophobic chain. The above results demonstrated that the constructed PEC sensor had good reproducibility, repeatability and stability, and was suitable for real application.

The practical applicability of the developed PEC sensor was verified by using FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs/ITO to test the cholesterol concentration in human serum sample. The serum sample was first diluted with ethyl acetate, and then the concentration of cholesterol was determined by the PEC sensor to be 3.41 × 10−3 mol/L, which matched the hospital test result (3.48 × 10−3 mol/L). Different amounts of cholesterol standard were added to the diluted serum sample to verify the practicability of the sensor by the recovery rate and relative standard deviation. The results were listed in Table 2 , showing that the recovery rate of cholesterol was 99.30 %−103.68 % and RSD were less than 4.59 %. The above results proved the feasibility of this method, and the high reliability of the new PEC sensor for actual sample analysis.

In sum, we selected perfluorinated organic compounds PFEA as one of the functional monomer to prepare FMIPs, which was utilized to functionalize CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs. PFEA not only endowed CH3NH3PbI3 specific selectivity, but also improved its water resistance owing to the fluorine containing long hydrophobic chain. FMIPs can dramatically enhance the charge transfer efficiency because the polymer passivated the grain boundary of CH3NH3PbI3 and lowered the defect density. Moreover, the introduction of FMIPs inhibited the recombination of electron holes and prolonged the carrier life time. By employing FMIPs@CH3NH3PbI3@CNDs, a new photoelectrochemical platform for cholesterol sensing in human serum samples was developed with simple preparation process, high stability and excellent selectivity. Our work demonstrated a novel strategy towards stable and efficient perovskite with FMIPs and expanded the development of MIPs-based sensors.
